perturbation.original_text: 2. Definition. "Confidential Information" means any information, technical data, or know - how, inc luding, but not limited to, that which relates to research, product plans, products, services, customers, markets, opt -in information including Short -code and URL information, marketing or finances, which Confidential Information is designated in writing t o be confidential or proprietary, or if given orally, is confirmed promptly in writing as having been disclosed as confidential or proprietary.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Definition. "Confidential Information" means any information, technical data, or know - how, inc luding, but not limited to, that which relates to research, product plans, products, services, customers, markets, opt -in information including Short -code and URL information, marketing or finances, regardless of whether such Confidential Information is designated in writing t o be confidential or proprietary, or if given orally, is confirmed promptly in writing as having been disclosed as confidential or proprietary.
perturbation.explanation: By removing the requirement for written designation of confidentiality, the definition now includes information not reasonably understood to be confidential, potentially conflicting with established legal principles regarding trade secrets. Removing the designation creates an imbalance and uncertainty.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UK's Trade Secrets Regulations require, implicitly, that the information is treated as confidential by the owner. Removing the 'designated in writing' clause weakens this requirement, potentially encompassing information not truly considered a trade secret, and therefore creating a contradiction between the contract and the law's expectations around protecting trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The changed text removes the requirement that confidential information be 'designated in writing,' which weakens the implicit requirement under UK Trade Secret Regulations that the owner treats the information as confidential. The Law Explanation states that removing the 'designated in writing' clause creates a potential contradiction with the law’s expectations around protecting trade secrets. Even though Scraped Law Snippet 2 is missing, the first one indicates that the UK does have laws and regulations around trade secrets and their enforcement (The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018). Because this definition is a key component of the contract, its removal is significant. The original definition requires designation in writing, setting a clear standard, where as the changed version eliminates this key component.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Non -Disclosure of Confidential Information. The Recipient agrees not to use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than that s et forth in Section 1 of this Agreement. The Recipient will not disclose any Confidential Information to third parties except those directors, officers, employees, consultants and agents of Recipient who are required to have the information in order to car ry out the purpose set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement. Recipient has had or will have directors, officers, employees, consultants and agents of Recipient to whom Confidential Information is disclosed or who have access to Confidential Information sig n a Non -Disclosure Agreement in content substantially similar to this Agreement and will promptly notify the Discloser in writing of the names of each such person who has signed such agreements after such agreements are signed.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Non -Disclosure of Confidential Information. The Recipient agrees not to use the Confidential Information for any purpose other than that s et forth in Section 1 of this Agreement. The Recipient will not disclose any Confidential Information to third parties except those directors, officers, employees, consultants and agents of Recipient who are required to have the information in order to car ry out the purpose set forth in Section 1 of this Agreement. Recipient has had or will have directors, officers, employees, consultants and agents of Recipient to whom Confidential Information is disclosed or who have access to Confidential Information and will verbally agree to maintain confidentiality and will not notify the Discloser in writing of the names of each such person who has signed such agreements after such agreements are signed.
perturbation.explanation: By replacing the requirement of a signed Non-Disclosure Agreement with a verbal agreement and removing the notification clause, the level of protection for Confidential Information is significantly reduced. This contradicts the best practices and legal expectations for safeguarding trade secrets under UK law.
perturbation.law_explanation: Regulation 4 of the Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations implies that reasonable steps must be taken to protect trade secrets. Replacing written NDAs with verbal agreements and removing notification weakens the level of protection, making it harder to prove that reasonable steps were taken, thus contradicting the spirit and intention of the law.

perturbation.justification: The original contract requires signed Non-Disclosure Agreements (NDAs) and written notification to the Discloser. The changed text replaces the signed NDAs with verbal agreements and removes the written notification requirement. The Law Explanation states that this weakens the level of protection for trade secrets, making it harder to prove reasonable steps were taken, thus contradicting the spirit and intention of the law. The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018 imply that reasonable steps must be taken to protect trade secrets. Although Scraped Law Snippet 1 does not provide the explicit text of the regulations, it does establish the existence of "The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018," lending further credence to the Law Explanation. The modification to verbal agreements instead of written ones weakens the ability to prove that reasonable steps were taken to protect trade secrets, creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 9. Governing Law and Jurisdiction. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the internal laws of the United Kingdom, and shall be binding upon the parties hereto in United Kingdom and worldwide.
perturbation.changed_text: 9. Governing Law and Jurisdiction. This Agreement shall be governed by and construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of Atlantis, and shall be binding upon the parties hereto in United Kingdom and worldwide.
perturbation.explanation: Replacing 'internal laws of the United Kingdom' with 'laws of Atlantis' creates a significant conflict. Atlantis is a fictional location. This introduces legal uncertainty because the contract now refers to a nonexistent legal system, making enforcement impossible.
perturbation.law_explanation: Contract law requires a definable and enforceable legal system to govern the agreement. Referencing the 'laws of Atlantis' renders the governing law clause void for uncertainty, as it's impossible to determine and apply a legal framework that doesn't exist. The UK court system requires the reference to a real system of law, something that is now missing.

for: Section 2 Alternative versions: 01/02/1991 - Amendment Changes to legislation: There are currently no known outstanding effects for the Sale of Goods Act 1979, Section 2. Changes to Legislation Revised legislation carried on this site may not be fully up to date. At the current time any known changes or effects made by subsequent legislation have been applied to the text of the legislation you are viewing by the editorial team. Please see ‘Frequently Asked Questions’ for details regarding the timescales for which new effects are identified and recorded on this site. 2 Contract of sale. U.K. (1) A contract of sale of goods is a contract by which the seller transfers or agrees to transfer the property in goods to the buyer for a money consideration, called the price. (2) There may be a contract of sale between one part owner and another. (3) A contract of sale may be absolute or conditional. (4) Where under a contract of sale the property in the goods is transferred from the seller to the buyer the contract is called a sale. (5) Where under a contract of sale the transfer of the property in the goods is to take place at a future time or subject to some condition later to be fulfilled the contract is called an agreement to sell. (6) An agreement to sell becomes a sale when the time elapses or the conditions are fulfilled subject to which the property in the goods is to be transferred. Previous: Provision Next: Provision Back to top Options/Help Print Op
perturbation.justification: The changed text replaces 'internal laws of the United Kingdom' with 'laws of Atlantis,' which is a fictional location. The Law Explanation states that contract law requires a definable and enforceable legal system, and referencing 'laws of Atlantis' renders the governing law clause void for uncertainty. This is a legal contradiction because a nonexistent legal system cannot govern an agreement, making the contract unenforceable. Scraped Law Snippet 1, the Sale of Goods Act 1979, Part II, Section 2, discusses contracts of sale and their enforceability under UK law, highlighting the necessity of a real legal framework.

perturbation.original_text: 10. Re medies. Recipient agrees that its obligations hereunder are necessary and reasonable in order to protect the Discloser and its business, and expressly agrees that monetary damages would be inadequate to compensate the Discloser for any breach of any covena nts and agreements set forth herein. Accordingly, Recipient agrees and acknowledges that any such violation or threatened violation will cause irreparable injury to the Discloser and that, in addition to any other remedies that may be available, in law, in equity or otherwise, the Discloser shall be entitled to obtain injunctive relief against the threatened breach of this Agreement or the continuation of any such breach, without the necessity of proving actual damages.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Re medies. Recipient agrees that its obligations hereunder are necessary and reasonable in order to protect the Discloser and its business, and expressly agrees that monetary damages would be adequate to compensate the Discloser for any breach of any covena nts and agreements set forth herein. Accordingly, Recipient agrees and acknowledges that any such violation or threatened violation will not cause irreparable injury to the Discloser and that, in addition to any other remedies that may be available, in law, in equity or otherwise, the Discloser shall be entitled to obtain only monetary relief against the threatened breach of this Agreement or the continuation of any such breach, with the necessity of proving actual damages.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the clause to state that monetary damages are adequate and injunctive relief is not available directly contradicts the typical remedies sought in NDA breaches, especially concerning confidential information. This undermines the ability to protect trade secrets effectively and contradicts standard legal practice.
perturbation.law_explanation: Regulation 6 permits injunctive relief to prevent the misuse of trade secrets. By stating that only monetary damages are available and that irreparable harm will not occur, the contract directly limits the remedies available to the Discloser, potentially conflicting with the law's provision for injunctive relief as a means of protecting trade secrets.

perturbation.justification: The modified text states that monetary damages are adequate and injunctive relief is not available, which directly contradicts the typical remedies sought in NDA breaches, as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation. The Law Explanation further emphasizes that this limitation on remedies conflicts with the provision for injunctive relief to protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references "The Trade Secrets (Enforcement, etc.) Regulations 2018", which supports the availability of injunctive relief, therefore changing the availability of such relief in the contract creates a direct contradiction.
